chunk_summary: The decision-maker mindset for B2B marketing: long sales cycles require convincing multiple stakeholders (user, manager, manager's manager, leadership). Marketing must handle objections for every level of the org chart. Create content that 'translates' when passed up the chain so champions can sell internally.
content: over the last decade the team and I at exposure ninja have generated over 100,000 B2B leads for ourselves and our clients B2B lead generation is its own Beast you might be dealing with really long sales cycles and selling to people who aren't actually the decision maker ... in B2B buying Cycles can be long this can be because of the cost but also because of the number of people that you need to convince in the organization we've had B2B clients where we've had to convince the user their manager their manager manager and Company leadership if any one of these people isn't convinced that this thing is a good thing to buy you don't get the sale we as marketers have a role to play in this ... here's someone on Reddit crying out for help how do I convince my co-workers to use Salesforce in this case this person's company has already been sold sales Force ... but for many of us we won't actually see people talking about these objections these are conversations that will be happening inside the business as marketers we need to be there handling these objections in our marketing ... we are often contacted by marketing managers that have read our books or listened to the podcast or watched a video about exposure ninja and they're really excited to work with us but when they take that decision back to their manager their manager says Ah we've tried agencies in the past it really hasn't worked so we have to make sure that our marketing material translates well enough to that person ... you need to make sure you understand the language that the ultimate decision maker is going to be using you also need to know what type of information they're going to need to get to a decision maybe case studies are going to be really beneficial for them or taking a free trial or having some sort of satisfaction guarantee and this approach is sort of a universal filter that we need to apply to every piece of marketing that we do
questions_on_this_chunk: Why are B2B sales cycles so long?
How do I market to multiple decision makers in a company?
What content should I create for different stakeholders?
How can I help a champion sell my product internally?

chunk_summary: Strategy 1 — Helpful content marketing: figure out who your target audience is and what information they need to achieve their goals (get promoted, make more money). Produce genuinely useful content that helps them do their job better. For B2B, this means content that ranks for the problems they're searching for.
content: let's talk about our first strategy cunningly titled being really helpful through content marketing so many businesses struggle with what to publish they know they should be publishing content information on their website information on their socials but they really don't know what to publish ... the overall plan with any content marketing should be figure out who our Target audiences and then figure out what information they need to get closer to their goals in B2B this is looking good and doing a really good job at work so they get promotion or they make their company more money for example we've got a client that sells takeaway packaging the stuff that restaurants use to put their food in when people order takeaways now the owners of takeaways aren't necessarily industry experts in takeaway packaging so the types of information that we've published on their website have been things like helping them get to grips with new regulations around the materials used in packaging or how to make sustainable packaging policies we're just trying to help that person do a better job in their business and of course when we talk about those things they match the sorts of things that our target audience is going to be searching for they find our website they see oh wow this is really useful information and oh they also sell the packaging great
questions_on_this_chunk: What type of content should I create for B2B marketing?
How do I figure out what information my B2B audience needs?
Can you give an example of helpful content for a B2B company?

chunk_summary: How to future-proof content for Google's AI overviews: tick the E-E-A-T boxes (Expertise, Experience, Authoritativeness, Trust). Share your direct experience — don't just publish generic information. Demonstrate author credentials, qualifications, and memberships on the page. Ranking in AI overviews can drive high-intent commercial traffic.
content: this type of content marketing has become more competitive as lots more businesses do it then of course we've got Google's AI overviews entering the fray with AI overviews Google is actually showing AI generated answers in its search results so answers to really basic questions are just going to be answered directly in the search results this doesn't mean that being helpful in writing the sort of content that your potential customers are going to search for is obsolete though we've seen how AI overviews can be really powerful at driving traffic through to websites if you're ranking in the AI overview panel and that traffic can have more commercial intent because it's already started doing some of the research actually in the search results the key to building a future proof content strategy is to make sure that you're ticking the eat boxes these are expertise experience authoritativeness and trust these are four principles shared by Google which it uses to denote good quality content ... the area that most B2B companies fall down on is sharing their direct experience in their content a lot of companies will just publish generic information which really doesn't make the most of the fact that this business has done a lot of work and has a lot of experience in the area it's also really important to demonstrate your knowledge and expertise on the page itself so for example here we've got a Blog post written for a client and this is a medical topic so we need to make sure that the client is shown as the author of this and we need to build their credibility through showing all of the different qualifications and the memberships that they have that demonstrates authority to readers and Google
questions_on_this_chunk: What is E-E-A-T in content marketing?
How do I optimize content for Google's AI overviews?
Why is showing author expertise important for SEO?
How can B2B companies share direct experience in content?

chunk_summary: Content format decisions depend on your audience and funnel stage: if customers know what they need, SEO-optimized website content works well. If they don't know they have a problem, use thought leadership, digital PR in trade publications, and definition content to create awareness — then dominate search for those new terms.
content: now you might be thinking Yeah Tim great we need to publish content but what type of content do we publish like if we're posting on our website does it need to be Longs that need to be short do we need to publish on social media do we need to make videos like you well your decision here really depends on your audience and the stages of the funnel that you're targeting ... targeting search by writing content on your website optimized for the keywords that your customers are searching for can be fantastic if people know what they're looking for if they know that they've got a problem but sometimes B2B businesses are selling something that customers don't know they need in which case you might need to try a different strategy for example we worked with a client for many years that sold HR software this software helped companies manage their employees performance but rather than doing this on a oneoff kpi thing it did it continually through the year the challenge is that businesses don't know that they need this right kpis are just a normal way of doing things so why would we need to change them so people weren't necessarily searching for what this business did so we got around this in three ways firstly we use thought leadership which we're actually going to come back to a little bit later on secondly we targeted the sorts of topics that their customers thought they needed help with so maybe they'd heard of a term like continuous Performance Management but they didn't really know what it was so we published definition content to help them get to grips with this New Concept but we also did some digital PR where we would go out to the sorts of Publications that this customer base was reading for example HR Publications and we'd publish articles with them about how to improve the performance of your employees ... in all of these articles we'd be seeding this topic of continuous Performance Management now because we'd already written the definition pieces and we got them ranked on Google when somebody went to Google and searched for continuous Performance Management up they found our client this generated tons of business for them
questions_on_this_chunk: What content format should I use for B2B marketing?
How do I market a product that customers don't know they need?
What is digital PR and how does it help B2B?
How do you create demand for a new concept in B2B?

chunk_summary: Lead magnets and paid traffic to free content: turn useful content into lead magnets (ebooks, brochures, guides). Run paid ads to free informational content when the audience is highly qualified — even if the download doesn't directly make money. Example: an office management software company ran ads to a hybrid working policy guide because it attracted exactly the right prospects.
content: sometimes the content that you create is good enough that you can actually make it a lead magnet meaning you can ask people for their name and email address in return for downloading it we did this for one of our B2B commercial cleaning clients putting together a brochure which they could offer on their website as a secondary call to action for people who weren't ready to get in contact yet so how do you take this being helpful through content marketing stuff to another level well one of the ways that you can do this is by actually running paid traffic to the free informational content that you've put out we've worked with a fantastic company that sells office management software so they help you manage who's coming in and going out of your office now during the pandemic obviously their world and their clients's world were turned completely upside down but post pandemic lots of their clients and potential clients had to think about something brand new how to create a hybrid working policy this was something that they'd never had to do before so what we did is we created a guide on how to produce a hybrid working policy this was really useful to their potential clients because they'd never had to do this before we could give them essentially a copy and paste policy that they could just use straight away and because of what this business sold we knew that people in that situation were a really good potential customer now as well as just publishing this on their website we also ran ads to it even though we weren't directly making money from the downloads of this guide we knew that the people interested in this was so qualified we will pay money to have them go and visit that page which is exactly what we did
questions_on_this_chunk: Should I run paid ads to free content?
What makes a good lead magnet for B2B?
How can I use a free guide to attract qualified leads?
When should I gate content behind an email form?

chunk_summary: Strategy 2 — Content repurposing blitz: create one original piece of content and tweak it for multiple channels (blog, video, podcast, talk, email, social). You can also repurpose the same core concept for different audience segments by adjusting the language and pain points for each stakeholder level (user, manager, leadership).
content: let's talk about strategy number two and that is a Content repurposing Blitz the reality is that content can be expensive and difficult to produce particularly if you're trying to produce content from multiple different channels your website LinkedIn YouTube X emails there's loads of different channels and each one requires a slightly different approach the good news is that with a lot of this stuff you can come up with an original piece of content and then tweak it optimize it and repurpose it for all of these different channels Let me Give an example so last year we gave some talks at a big B2B marketing event now we'd written a blog post which we then turned into a video and a podcast which we then turned into the talk at the event so we generated hundreds of leads from the talks at that event without having to actually create anything original because we'd already done it or sometimes we'll work with a client that's used direct mail and we'll be able to take a direct mail campaign that's been really effective and turn that into a drip email sequence or maybe there's an internal sales guide that helps your sales team talk about a certain topic well maybe that could be repurposed and turned into a Blog by taking this repurposing approach you're able to publish content in different places without multiplying the amount of work involved ... now as well as repurposing content for different channels you can also repurpose content for different segments of your audience remember earlier we talked about needing to sell to the end user and their manager and their manager manager and Company leadership where you can produce webinars or white papers or guides for each of these different levels of the company taking an original concept and then just tweaking it to use the language the pain points and the desires of the person at that level in the company
questions_on_this_chunk: How can I repurpose one piece of content across multiple channels?
What is a content repurposing blitz?
How do I adapt content for different stakeholders in a company?
Can I turn a sales guide into a blog post?

chunk_summary: Strategy 3 — Thought leadership (redefined): give your company a human face that people latch onto. Position individuals in your business as experts. People connect with people more than brands. Avoid the trap of copying competitors (e.g., everyone publishing white papers) — calibrate to what customers actually want. Even mundane, authentic content can build connection.
content: strategy number three is thought leadership now let's talk about thought leadership I know that that term can bring up pictures of 21y old life coaches and shouty public speaking gigs but that's really not what I'm talking about when we talk about thought leadership we're really talking about showing up in the places that your audience is looking for help being helpful and human some of the most effective thought leadership can be about giving your company a human face that people can latch on to because people latch on to individuals more than they do to a company that's kind of why I'm here on our YouTube channel because hopefully I know my stuff and I can share it in a way that connects with exposure ninja's audience going back to the HR software example earlier we worked with the founder of that company to build their personal brand around the topics that their company was an expert in because we positioned them as a thought leader we were able to get them visibility in all sorts of Publications getting in front of good quality Target customers for them in a way that felt human he could talk about his experience and stories from being at different organizations and how the Performance Management was handled and that was much more compelling than hey we're a company and we've got something to sell you but you do need to be aware of a bit of a thought leadership trap which is that in some Industries lots of the businesses just copy each other they think that thought leadership means putting out white papers and everyone then puts out white papers but actually if you talk to the customers in that industry they never really read the white papers they much prefer a short personal email instead so you need to make sure that your strategy here is calibrated to what your customers actually want than to anybody's ego or just copying what competitors are doing ... it's about having the balance of making things interesting for the sake of the actually genuinely interesting content and then making things authentic enough that it can be every day and still achievable ... sometimes it's the boring and mundane that actually makes things seem achievable and I think if a customer feels like something's been achievable for someone it could be achievable for them
questions_on_this_chunk: What is thought leadership in B2B marketing?
How do I build a personal brand for my B2B company?
Should I publish white papers for thought leadership?
How can authentic content help with B2B sales?

chunk_summary: Advanced thought leadership: use a founder's personal brand to get digital PR coverage on major publications (Inc, Forbes, trade journals), earn backlinks, boost SEO rankings, and dominate target keywords. The HR software client's campaign was still dominant 9 years later. Not everyone is camera-ready — find people in your company who are comfortable on camera.
content: thought leadership also doesn't mean just one person in the business after all what happens if I'm crushed by a giant rubber chicken it's likely that you'll have different experts in different areas in your business they might need a bit of help getting their expertise out to your audience so you might need to interview them or make a video or podcast with them ... one piece of advice here it really helps if you've got people that are engaging you'd have no idea the number of people I've spoken to that are more than happy to stand up in front of a room of their employees and talk about whatever but get them in front of a camera and they just shut down completely so you do need people that are fairly comfortable in front of a camera if you're going to be videoing them you do need people who are happy talking on a microphone if you're going to be using them in podcasts to me that is as important as the information that they're putting out if you want to take this to another level you can actually use thought leadership as a fantastic way to build links and get digital PR coverage remember the HR software company that I've spoken about throughout so we use that Founders thought leadership and personal brand to get loads of coverage on sites like Inc Forbes and a whole bunch of HR trade publications because they knew about the founder we built their brand into something that was well known in their space which meant that we could get articles published we could get links back to their website which really helped boost their visibility that helped their website's ranking and it helped them dominate for their main target keyword which 9 years after starting that campaign they are still dominant for today
questions_on_this_chunk: How can thought leadership help with SEO and backlinks?
How do I get featured in major publications like Forbes?
What if my internal experts aren't comfortable on camera?
How long does it take to see results from thought leadership?
